preamble.summary: A victory hymn celebrating God's defense of Zion. God is known in Judah, His dwelling is in Salem (Jerusalem), and from Zion He has shattered the weapons of war — the flashing arrows, the shields, the swords, the battle itself. The warriors who came against the city lie stunned and motionless, unable even to lift their hands. The psalm declares that God's rebuke from heaven silences the earth, that He rises in judgment to save the humble, and that even human wrath serves His purposes. All nations are called to bring tribute to the One who is terrifying to kings.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 76 presents God as a warrior of terrifying stillness. He does not fight in the conventional sense — He rebukes, and warriors fall asleep. The 'sleep' of verse 6 (nirdemu, 'they were put into a deep sleep') is not death but paralysis: the enemy soldiers cannot function. God wins by overwhelming the enemy's capacity to act. This is holy war reduced to its theological essence: God does not need Israel's army; He needs only to speak. The psalm may reflect the miraculous deliverance of Jerusalem from Sennacherib's siege (2 Kings 19:35), when the Assyrian army was destroyed overnight without a battle.
preamble.friction: The name Salem (Shalem) in verse 3 is an archaic name for Jerusalem, appearing elsewhere only in Genesis 14:18 (Melchizedek, king of Salem). Its use here is deliberately archaic, evoking Jerusalem's pre-Israelite identity as a city of ancient holiness. The phrase sukko ('His shelter/lair') in the same verse uses a word that can mean 'booth' (as in Sukkot) or 'lair' (as of a lion) — the ambiguity is productive, presenting God simultaneously as tent-dweller and predator.
preamble.connections: The deliverance of Zion connects to the Zion theology of Psalms 46, 48, and 87 — the conviction that God's presence in Jerusalem makes the city inviolable. The defeat of weapons (verse 4) parallels Psalm 46:10 ('He breaks the bow and shatters the spear'). The phrase nora attah ('You are terrifying') connects to the broader theme of divine terror in Psalm 68:36 and Habakkuk 3:2. The historical background likely involves the Assyrian crisis of 701 BCE (Isaiah 36-37, 2 Kings 18-19).

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician on Neginoth, A Psalm or Song of Asaph.
verses.rendering: For the director of music. With stringed instruments. A psalm of Asaph. A song.
translator_notes: The term binginot ('with stringed instruments') indicates accompaniment by the nevel (harp) and kinnor (lyre). The combination of mizmor and shir suggests both instrumental and vocal performance.

verses.text_hebrew: נוֹדָ֣ע בִּֽיהוּדָ֣ה אֱלֹהִ֑ים בְּ֝יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל גָּד֥וֹל שְׁמֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: In Judah is God known: his name is great in Israel.
verses.rendering:
God is known in Judah;
His name is great in Israel.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with a statement of divine fame. The verb noda ('is known') is passive — God has made Himself known through His acts. In Judah specifically, and in Israel broadly, God's shem ('name, reputation') is gadol ('great'). The name is not merely a label but a distillation of everything God has done. Judah and Israel may represent the southern and northern kingdoms respectively, or they may be parallel terms for the whole covenant people.
translator_notes: The pairing of Judah and Israel may suggest a pre-schism or reunification context, or it may simply be poetic parallelism using two names for the same people.

verses.text_kjv: In Salem also is his tabernacle, and his dwelling place in Zion.
verses.rendering:
His shelter is in Salem,
and His dwelling place in Zion.
translator_notes: The name Shalem may be related to shalom ('peace'), making Jerusalem 'the city of peace' — an ironic name given its violent history. The psalmist may be playing on this: in the city of peace, the God of peace destroys the weapons of war.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁ֣מָּה שִׁ֭בַּר רִשְׁפֵי־קָ֑שֶׁת מָגֵ֬ן וְחֶ֖רֶב וּמִלְחָמָ֣ה סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: There brake he the arrows of the bow, the shield, and the sword, and the battle. Selah.
verses.rendering:
There He shattered the flashing arrows of the bow,
the shield, the sword, and the battle itself. Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word shammah ('there') points to Zion — the very place where the divine warrior acts. The verb shibbar ('He shattered, He broke to pieces') describes violent destruction of military hardware. The rishpei qashet ('fiery arrows, flashing bolts of the bow') are the most advanced ranged weapons of the ancient world. The magen ('shield'), cherev ('sword'), and milchamah ('war, battle') complete the catalogue. But the final item is the most striking: God shattered not just weapons but milchamah — war itself. He did not merely win the battle; He abolished it.
translator_notes: The word reshef can mean 'flame, spark, arrow' and is also the name of a Canaanite deity of plague and war. The psalmist may be deliberately claiming that YHWH defeats not only human weapons but the divine forces the enemies believed were behind them.

key_terms.semantic_range: sparks, flames, fiery bolts, arrows; also a Canaanite war deity (Resheph)
key_terms.note: Resheph as a common noun means 'flame' or 'burning arrow.' As a divine name, Resheph was the Canaanite god of plague and warfare. The psalmist's use of the term may carry both meanings: God shatters the physical arrows and the spiritual power behind them.

verses.text_kjv: Thou art more glorious and excellent than the mountains of prey.
verses.rendering:
Radiant are You, majestic,
more than the mountains of prey.
verses.expanded_rendering: The adjective na'or ('radiant, luminous, glorious') describes God as a source of light — He shines. The word addir ('majestic, mighty, noble') adds weight to the luminosity. The phrase me-harerei taref ('more than the mountains of prey') is mysterious: it may refer to mountains where predatory animals hunt, or to the mountains where enemy armies set up camps (fortified heights from which they launch raids), or metaphorically to the hostile powers themselves. God outshines them all.
translator_notes: The phrase harerei taref is unique in the Hebrew Bible. Some emend taref ('prey') to ad ('eternity') or ered ('I descend'), but the Masoretic reading is defensible: God is more majestic than the predatory mountains — more dangerous, more powerful, more commanding than the heights where eagles and lions hunt.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶשְׁתּ֘וֹלְל֤וּ ׀ אַבִּ֣ירֵי לֵ֭ב נָמ֣וּ שְׁנָתָ֑ם וְלֹא־מָצְא֖וּ כׇל־אַנְשֵׁי־חַ֣יִל יְדֵיהֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: The stouthearted are spoiled, they have slept their sleep: and none of the men of might have found their hands.
verses.rendering:
The warriors of bold heart were plundered;
they sank into their sleep,
and none of the soldiers
could lift their hands.
verses.expanded_rendering: The abbirei lev ('bold-hearted, stout-hearted warriors') — the bravest soldiers the enemy could field — were eshtolelu ('plundered, stripped, despoiled'). The phrase namu shenatam ('they slept their sleep') describes a supernatural stupor: they fell into a sleep from which they could not rouse themselves. The final phrase velo mats'u kol anshei chayil yedeihem ('none of the warriors found their hands') is devastating: they could not even locate their own hands to pick up weapons. God's intervention reduced elite soldiers to helpless bodies.
translator_notes: If this psalm reflects the Sennacherib event of 701 BCE, 2 Kings 19:35 reports that 'the angel of the LORD went out and struck down 185,000 in the Assyrian camp.' The psalmist describes the same event in poetic terms: the soldiers 'slept' and could not fight.

verses.text_hebrew: מִ֭גַּעֲרָ֣תְךָ אֱלֹהֵ֣י יַעֲקֹ֑ב נִ֝רְדָּ֗ם וְרֶ֣כֶב וָסֽוּס׃
verses.text_kjv: At thy rebuke, O God of Jacob, both the chariot and horse are cast into a dead sleep.
verses.rendering:
At Your rebuke, O God of Jacob,
both rider and horse lay stunned.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word ge'aratekha ('Your rebuke, Your roar') is the sound God makes when He intervenes — a thunderous shout that paralyzes everything in its path. The rekhev vasus ('chariot and horse') represent the ancient world's most feared military technology — cavalry and chariot corps. Both are nirdamu ('put into a deep sleep, stunned into unconsciousness'). The God of Jacob does not need chariots of His own; His voice alone neutralizes the enemy's entire armored division.
translator_notes: The verb nirdam ('was put into deep sleep') is related to tardemah, the deep sleep God imposed on Adam in Genesis 2:21 and on Abram in Genesis 15:12. It is a divinely induced unconsciousness — not natural sleep but supernatural incapacitation.

verses.text_hebrew: אַתָּ֤ה ׀ נ֥וֹרָא אַ֗תָּה וּמִי־יַעֲמֹ֥ד לְפָנֶ֗יךָ מֵאָ֥ז אַפֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou, even thou, art to be feared: and who may stand in thy sight when once thou art angry?
verses.rendering:
You — You are to be feared!
Who can stand before You
when Your anger burns?
translator_notes: The doubled attah... attah ('You... You') is emphatic — the psalmist points directly at God with awe. The rhetorical question 'who can stand?' (mi ya'amod lefanekha) expects the answer 'no one.' When God's af ('anger, nose, wrath') is aroused, every human position collapses.

key_terms.note: Nora is from yare ('to fear') and describes that which inspires terror, awe, or reverence. When applied to God, it means God is the most terrifying reality in the universe — not because He is cruel but because He is absolute.

verses.text_hebrew: מִ֭שָּׁמַיִם הִשְׁמַ֣עְתָּ דִּ֑ין אֶ֖רֶץ יָרְאָ֣ה וְשָׁקָֽטָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou didst cause judgment to be heard from heaven; the earth feared, and was still,
verses.rendering:
From heaven You proclaimed judgment;
the earth feared and fell silent,
translator_notes: The verb hishma'ta ('You caused to be heard, You proclaimed') indicates that God's judgment was audible — it came as a sound from heaven. The earth's response is twofold: yar'ah ('it feared') and shaqatah ('it fell silent, it was still'). Terror produces silence — the entire created order holds its breath when God speaks judgment.

verses.text_kjv: When God arose to judgment, to save all the meek of the earth. Selah.
verses.rendering:
when God rose for judgment,
to save all the humble of the earth. Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The purpose of God's terrifying intervention is now revealed: lehoshia kol anvei erets ('to save all the humble of the earth'). The judgment that terrifies kings and paralyzes armies serves a specific end — the rescue of the anvavim ('humble, lowly, afflicted'). This is the consistent biblical pattern: God's power is not exercised for its own sake but for the sake of the powerless. The selah provides a pause to absorb this connection between terror and tenderness.
translator_notes: The anvavim ('humble ones') are those who have been bowed down by oppression and who wait for God's deliverance. They are the theological heirs of the aniyyim ('afflicted') in Psalm 72:2, 4, 12-14.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־חֲמַ֣ת אָ֭דָם תּוֹדֶ֑ךָּ שְׁאֵרִ֖ית חֵמֹ֣ת תַּחְגֹּֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Surely the wrath of man shall praise thee: the remainder of wrath shalt thou restrain.
verses.rendering:
For human wrath will praise You;
the remnant of wrath You will wear as a belt.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is one of the most theologically dense verses in the Psalter. The claim chamat adam todekka ('human wrath praises You') means that even human rage, the most destructive force in the social world, is co-opted by God for His own glory. When nations attack in fury, their very attack becomes the occasion for God to display His power — and that display produces praise. The second line — she'erit chemot tachgor ('the remnant of wrath You will bind on') — pictures God wearing the leftover wrath like a belt or garment. Even the residual energy of human anger is claimed by God as an accessory.
translator_notes: This verse has been variously translated, with some reading tachgor as 'You will restrain' rather than 'You will gird Yourself with.' The image of God wearing human wrath as a belt is striking: it means nothing escapes God's sovereignty, not even the forces arrayed against Him.

verses.text_hebrew: נִדְר֣וּ וְ֭שַׁלְּמוּ לַיהוָ֣ה אֱלֹהֵיכֶ֑ם כׇּל־סְ֝בִיבָ֗יו יוֹבִ֥ילוּ שַׁ֣י לַמּוֹרָֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: Vow, and pay unto the LORD your God: let all that be round about him bring presents unto him that ought to be feared.
translator_notes: The imperative nidru ve-shallemu ('vow and pay') calls for fulfilled commitment — promises to God must be kept. The phrase kol sevivav ('all around Him') includes not just Israelites but all surrounding nations. The word shai ('tribute, gift') is the same term used for the gifts brought to Solomon (Psalm 72:10). The designation la-mora ('to the feared One, to the Awesome One') closes the psalm on the note of terror that has been its consistent theme.

verses.text_kjv: He shall cut off the spirit of princes: he is terrible to the kings of the earth.
verses.rendering:
He cuts off the spirit of rulers;
He is terrifying to the kings of the earth.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb yivtsor ('He cuts off, He harvests, He restrains') pictures God pruning the ru'ach ('spirit, breath, wind') of negidim ('rulers, princes, leaders'). God does not merely defeat kings; He deflates them — He takes the breath and bravado out of them. The final phrase nora lemalkhei arets ('terrifying to the kings of the earth') extends the scope beyond Israel's immediate enemies to all earthly power. No king anywhere is exempt from God's sovereignty. The psalm that began with God known in Judah ends with God feared everywhere.
translator_notes: The verb batsar ('to cut off, to harvest grapes, to fortify') here means 'to clip, to restrain, to cut short.' The image is of a vintner cutting grape clusters — God harvests the arrogance of rulers as easily as cutting fruit from a vine.
